messages.content: mc 1.4.7 release date
The release date for Minecraft version 1.4.7 was on December 27th, 2012.
when was mc 1.17.1 released
Minecraft version 1.17.1 was released on July 6th, 2021.
mc 1.16.5 release date
Minecraft version 1.16.5 was released on January 15, 2021.
first minecraft version of 2020
The first Minecraft version of 2020 was version 1.15.2 released on January 21, 2020.

The first Minecraft version of 2019 was version 1.14 which was released on April 23, 2019.
first mc version ever released
The first Minecraft version ever released was an alpha version called "Minecraft Alpha v1.0.17_04" which was released on May 17, 2009.
Minecraft Alpha v1.0.17_04 is a version from 2010

I apologize for the mistake in my previous response. The first Minecraft version ever released was actually "Minecraft Classic 0.0.11a" which was released on May 17, 2009. This was the earliest playable version of the game and was distributed for free on the official Minecraft website.
first multiplayer version of mc

The first Minecraft version to have multiplayer functionality was Minecraft Classic 0.0.14a, which was released on June 9, 2009. This version allowed players to connect to each other's servers and play together over the internet.
first ever online multiplayer game

The first-ever online multiplayer game is a bit difficult to determine since the development of multiplayer capabilities first began in the 1960s. However, one of the earliest examples of an online multiplayer game is "MUD1" (Multi User Dungeon) which was launched in 1978. It was a text-based adventure game that allowed multiple players to connect to a server and play in the same virtual world together.
how did people connect to the internet to play MUD in 1978 when internet was invented in 1990

I apologize for my previous response as it was incorrect. The Internet was actually invented in the late 1960s, and the first online multiplayer game, MUD1, was developed in 1978. However, the Internet was not available to the public until the late 1980s and early 1990s. 

In order to play MUD1 in 1978, players would have needed access to the ARPANET (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network), which was a precursor to the Internet and was used primarily by government agencies and academic institutions. They would have needed to connect to the ARPANET through a computer terminal with a dial-up modem, and then connect to MUD1 through the terminal. Playing MUD1 was a highly technical process that required a lot of knowledge and expertise in computer systems and networking.
how did people get access to ARPANET

Access to ARPANET was initially restricted to the United States Department of Defense, government agencies, and select research institutions. However, over time access was granted to more organizations and universities for research purposes. 

To gain access to ARPANET, organizations had to apply and be approved by the Department of Defense's Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA). Once approved, the organization would need to establish a connection to the network using specialized equipment and software. 

Users who had access to ARPANET would typically connect to the network through a computer terminal and a dial-up modem. The terminal would then be used to run specialized software and access various services and resources available on the network.

It's worth noting that the ARPANET was eventually replaced by the modern Internet in the late 1980s and early 1990s, which significantly expanded public access to online services and resources.
is arpanet still around

No, ARPANET is not around anymore. ARPANET was decommissioned in 1990 and replaced by the modern Internet we know today. However, many of the technologies and protocols originally developed for ARPANET continue to be used in modern networks and the Internet, including TCP/IP, which is the fundamental protocol that allows data to be transmitted across the internet. So, while the ARPANET itself is no longer active or in use, its legacy continues to shape modern computer networking and internet technologies.
what would happen if someone tried to connect to arpanet using a 300 baud modem in 2023

It's not possible to connect to the ARPANET today as it was officially shut down in 1990. While the ARPANET's infrastructure formed the basis of what is now the modern internet, its hardware and software have long since been replaced or decommissioned. 

Even if someone had a 300 baud modem and tried to connect to the ARPANET's former infrastructure, they would not be successful as the network no longer exists. The physical equipment and software required to connect to the ARPANET are no longer in use, and modern networks use different technologies and protocols that would not be compatible with that type of equipment. In other words, it would not be possible to connect to the ARPANET even with older equipment.
300 baud modems

300 baud modems were an early type of modem that allowed computers to communicate over telephone lines at data rates of up to 300 bits per second (or 0.3 kilobits per second). They were commonly used in the 1970s and early 1980s for connecting personal computers and other systems to the early computer networks like ARPANET. 

While 300 baud modems may seem extremely slow by today's standards, they were groundbreaking at the time. They enabled users to connect to the internet and other computer networks at relatively fast speeds, and they played a critical role in the development of online communication, collaboration, and data sharing.

Today, 300 baud modems are no longer used, as faster and more efficient communication technologies like DSL, cable, and fiber optic internet have replaced them. However, they remain a symbol of the early days of computer networking and of the rapid pace of technological change.
